---
title: "AI Literacy in Education"
---

![](../static/logo_3.png)

In the fall 2025 semester, the Provost of St. Olaf College, Dr. Tarshia Stanley, held a series of “Provost Collaborations” exploring different themes to enable her to connect with and better understand the community she was joining. One of those collabs was a book club discussion around the book AI Snake Oil. That conversation drew students, faculty, and staff from across the campus to sit together and reflect on how AI, and specifically generative AI (gen AI), could/was already playing a role in education. At the end of that conversation, she challenged us all to go out and have a conversation with at least one person about gen AI and the classroom - a commission of sorts, if you will. I took her challenge very seriously. 

This AI literacy in education series was born out of my looking for a way to carry out that work of engaging our learning community in critical conversation around the role of gen AI in the present and future of our teaching and learning. 

As AI tools become increasingly present in academic life, faculty and students alike are navigating new questions about how these technologies should be used (or not) and regulated in classroom settings. This project creates a shared space for dialogue, recognizing that meaningful AI literacy requires not only technical knowledge but also thoughtful reflection on teaching pedagogy, ethics, and personal and institutional values.

By centering both student and faculty perspectives, the overarching goal of this project is to support more intentional, transparent, and equitable decisions about how generative AI is used in teaching and learning at St. Olaf. 

## What are my views 

If you are going to use this resource, I think that it is important to understand my personal views on the topic. I worked very hard to make sure that all the material across all of the sessions were neutral. However, I am not perfect. There is a good chance some of my biases may have creeped in and so it’s important that you know what those biases are. 

In my opinion, very few things in life are mutually exclusive. On the one hand, I despise that I have to be educated in an age where genAI tools are so prevalent and are showing up everywhere around us. On the other hand, I have seen how people have used these tools to be innovative and to accomplish things that they either couldn’t before or that had prohibitively high barriers to entry. I believe genAI tools are at the end of the day, just tools. And like every other tool, I believe we need to be taught how to use them well. The danger to the teaching anbd learning process comes not from the inclusion of these tools but from either including them in unstructured ways, prohibiting people from using then without addressing the why behind your decision, or any policy that does not invite dialogue and understanding. This is when people wil in fact turn to them in ways that are harmful to the teaching and learning process and dilute the goal of education. It is my strong belief that it is the responsibility of the institution to in some way shape or form, teach students how to leverage these tools, for what tasks it is appropriate to leverage them, potential pitfalls and how to identify them, and perhaps most importantly how an individual can assess when they are competent enough in a certain area to bring a genAI tool into their workflow. 

## A note on GenAI use

This website was built with the assistance of generative AI tools. Specifically, I used Claude AI (Sonnet 4.6 Adaptive) to help with formatting content in Quarto Markdown. 

All ideas, arguments, written content, and decisions reflected here are my own. GenAI tools were not used for the generation of any written content of any kind. 


**For any questions related to this project please contact Fortress Okorie at okorie1@stolaf.edu.**






[People](people.qmd) and [Resources](resources.qmd)


![](../static/logo_1.png)
